_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Henry County, VA. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Formed 1777 from Pittsylvania, with Martinsville as county seat. Henry County sits at the western edge of the Southside, where the Piedmont meets the Blue Ridge foothills. The county transitioned from Scots-Irish frontier settlement through tobacco farming to furniture and textile manufacturing centered on Martinsville.

current_courthouse.location: Martinsville, VA — East Main Street (modern judicial center; historic courthouse building also on courthouse square)

previous_courthouses.location: Henry Court House (later Martinsville) — early log courthouse on the original courthouse square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by a more substantial structure as population grew. The village that grew around the courthouse became Martinsville, named for Joseph Martin, a frontier leader.

previous_courthouses.location: Martinsville — brick courthouse on courthouse square
previous_courthouses.fate: Served for over a century. Suffered a significant fire in 1882 that damaged some records. Replaced by the current 1929 structure.

record_losses.description: A fire in 1882 damaged the Henry County courthouse and destroyed an undetermined number of records. The bound deed books and will books were largely saved, but court order books and loose papers suffered losses. The extent of destruction is debated — some sources describe the loss as significant, while others suggest that the most critical bound volumes were rescued. The fire primarily affected loose papers and some unbound records. The deed and will book series, while not unscathed, remained substantially intact from the county's 1777 formation.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1882 fire in Henry County created a selective gap — {ancestor_name}'s land deeds probably survived, but the court orders that would have placed {pronoun_object} on a road crew, documented an apprenticeship, or recorded a complaint may be gone. The records that survive document property; the records that burned documented daily life.
record_losses.source: Pedigo, Virginia G., and Lewis G. Pedigo. History of Patrick and Henry Counties, Virginia. Roanoke: Stone Printing and Manufacturing, 1933.

deeds.completeness: Good — bound deed books survived the 1882 fire largely intact. Continuous from county formation.
deeds.gaps: Pre-1777 deeds for the same territory filed under Pittsylvania County.
deeds.repository: Henry County Clerk of Court, Martinsville; Library of Virginia, Richmond
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com VA land records; Library of Virginia digital collections

wills_estates.completeness: Good for bound will books. Loose estate papers have gaps due to the 1882 fire.
wills_estates.gaps: Some loose estate papers lost in the 1882 fire. Bound will books substantially intact.
wills_estates.repository: Henry County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections

marriages.completeness: Moderate — some early marriage bonds were damaged or destroyed in the 1882 fire. More continuous after 1850.
marriages.gaps: Gaps in the pre-1882 bond series. Church records are essential supplements.
marriages.repository: Henry County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

court_minutes.completeness: Moderate — some court order books survived the 1882 fire, others did not. More continuous after 1882.
court_minutes.gaps: Gaps in the pre-1882 series due to fire damage.
court_minutes.repository: Henry County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

vital_records.completeness: Virginia state registration began 1912. Earlier vital events from church records and county court orders.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records; Library of Virginia for delayed certificates

church_records.parish: Patrick Parish (Church of England / Episcopal) — though Presbyterian and Baptist congregations were more significant in this frontier county
church_records.completeness: Anglican parish records are sparse — the frontier population was predominantly Presbyterian and Baptist. Presbyterian session records and Baptist church minutes are the primary church sources. The Scots-Irish settlers who dominated Henry County's early population were overwhelmingly Presbyterian.
church_records.repository: Library of Virginia; Presbyterian Historical Society; individual church archives
church_records.online_access: Limited published transcriptions; Library of Virginia microfilm

substitute_sources.description: Henry County was formed from Pittsylvania in 1777. Records for the Henry territory before 1777 are filed under Pittsylvania County.
substitute_sources.repository: Pittsylvania County Clerk of Court, Chatham; Library of Virginia
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Henry County families predating 1777. The Pittsylvania records cover the frontier period when Scots-Irish families were first settling the upper Dan River country.
substitute_sources.source: Clement, Maud Carter. The History of Pittsylvania County, Virginia. Lynchburg: J. P. Bell Company, 1929.

substitute_sources.source_type: Martinsville furniture and textile company records
substitute_sources.description: Martinsville became a major furniture and textile manufacturing center in the late nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Bassett Furniture, American Furniture Company, DuPont, and multiple textile mills generated employment records. Survival is uneven.
substitute_sources.repository: Martinsville-Henry County Historical Society; Bassett Historical Center; Library of Virginia
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: For families connected to the furniture or textile industries, company records may document employment, housing, and community life. The Bassett Historical Center in Bassett, VA, is particularly valuable for furniture industry families.
substitute_sources.source: Macy, Beth. Factory Man: How One Furniture Maker Battled Offshoring, Stayed Local — and Helped Save an American Town. New York: Little, Brown and Company, 2014.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Henry County enumerated from the first federal census.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. The 1790 census for Henry County documents the Scots-Irish frontier population. Manufacturing schedules (1820-1880) document early industrial activity including tanneries and distilleries.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Henry County, VA.

narrative_hooks: The 1882 courthouse fire in Henry County destroyed the records most likely to document daily life — court orders, estate inventories, loose papers — while the deed books that recorded property survived. {ancestor_name}'s land is documented; {pronoun_possessive} daily existence may not be.

Henry County was Scots-Irish frontier — the families who settled here in the 1770s were Presbyterian, not Anglican, and the church records that matter are session minutes and baptismal registers from congregations that had no connection to the established church.

Before 1777, Henry was Pittsylvania territory, and before that it was Halifax — tracking {ancestor_name}'s family to its origins in this part of the Southside means following the records backward through three county formations.

Martinsville became a furniture town in the twentieth century, and if {ancestor_name} worked at Bassett or one of the other companies, the factory records may document a life that the courthouse never captured — the daily clock-in, the housing assignment, the payroll entry.

Henry County sits where the Piedmont meets the mountains, and {ancestor_name}'s family may have come from either direction — east from the tobacco plantations or south down the Great Wagon Road from Pennsylvania through the Valley of Virginia.
source_references: Pedigo, Virginia G., and Lewis G. Pedigo. History of Patrick and Henry Counties, Virginia. Roanoke: Stone Printing and Manufacturing, 1933.
Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.
Macy, Beth. Factory Man: How One Furniture Maker Battled Offshoring, Stayed Local — and Helped Save an American Town. New York: Little, Brown and Company, 2014.
Clement, Maud Carter. The History of Pittsylvania County, Virginia. Lynchburg: J. P. Bell Company, 1929.
